title: Laryngospasm After LMA Placement
summary: A 24-year-old smoker with a recent upper respiratory infection develops partial and then complete laryngospasm shortly after LMA placement for a knee arthroscopy. Learners must recognize the obstruction, apply CPAP with jaw thrust, deepen the anesthetic, and escalate to succinylcholine and intubation if refractory.
learningObjectives: Identify risk factors for laryngospasm (recent URI, smoking, light anesthesia, airway manipulation, secretions).
Differentiate partial from complete laryngospasm using capnography, auscultation, and chest movement.
Perform first-line management: 100% oxygen, CPAP, jaw thrust with pressure at Larson's point, and removal of stimulus.
Escalate appropriately: deepen anesthesia with propofol, then give succinylcholine and secure the airway if the spasm is refractory.
Anticipate and manage complications of laryngospasm, including hypoxic bradycardia and negative pressure pulmonary edema.
Demonstrate clear crisis communication and early calls for help during an airway emergency.
setup: OR set up for a general anesthetic with LMA: size 4 LMA, backup size 3 and 5, functioning laryngoscope and 7.0 ETT immediately available.
Labeled syringes: propofol (both induction dose and a 'deepening' aliquot), succinylcholine 20 mg/mL, atropine 0.4 mg/mL.
Suction on and within reach under the pillow; manikin configured to allow jaw thrust and CPAP.
Program or simulate stridor audio for the partial laryngospasm phase and silent chest for the complete phase.
Chart documents a URI 2 weeks ago (resolved cough) and a half-pack-per-day smoking history.
Confederate circulating nurse available to fetch drugs and call for help when asked.

patient.name: Tyler Nguyen
medications: Ibuprofen as needed for knee pain
pmh: Upper respiratory infection 2 weeks ago, now resolved except occasional dry cough
Smoker, half pack per day for 6 years
Right ACL tear (sports injury), otherwise healthy
No prior anesthetics; no family anesthetic problems

airway.notes: Easy-appearing airway. Recent URI and smoking increase airway reactivity risk.

patient.plan: Right knee arthroscopy under general anesthesia with a size 4 LMA, spontaneous ventilation, sevoflurane maintenance.

phases.label: Induction
phases.description: IV induction with propofol; preparing for LMA insertion.

phases.label: LMA Placement
phases.description: LMA inserted and ventilation confirmed; volatile started.

phases.label: Laryngospasm
phases.description: Partial then complete laryngospasm with desaturation.

phases.label: Management
phases.description: CPAP, deepening, succinylcholine, and airway rescue.

events.label: Propofol induction given
events.description: Propofol 2-2.5 mg/kg IV. Depth falls to a surgical level, blood pressure drops modestly, respiratory rate slows.

events.label: LMA placed, ventilation confirmed
events.description: Size 4 LMA seated on first attempt; capnography confirms a good waveform with spontaneous ventilation.

events.label: Sevoflurane started at 2%
events.description: Sevoflurane dialed to 2% inspired; end-tidal rises toward 1.8% over several minutes. Depth is still relatively light when the airway is stimulated.

events.label: Partial laryngospasm
events.description: Inspiratory stridor with rocking chest movement. SpO2 drifts down slowly, EtCO2 waveform shrinks, respiratory effort increases. Triggered by light anesthesia and airway irritation.

events.label: Complete laryngospasm
events.description: Cords fully adducted: silent chest, no capnograph trace, no air movement despite effort. SpO2 falls rapidly through the 80s into the 70s. Fire if first-line measures are delayed or fail.

events.label: Bradycardia from hypoxia
events.description: Sustained hypoxia produces sinus bradycardia into the 40s — a pre-arrest sign. Faculty-triggered if hypoxia is prolonged.

events.label: Response to 100% O2 + CPAP + jaw thrust
events.description: Firm jaw thrust with pressure at Larson's point and sustained CPAP on 100% oxygen partially breaks the spasm: some air movement returns and SpO2 rises modestly.

events.label: Response to deepening with propofol
events.description: Propofol 0.5-1 mg/kg IV deepens the anesthetic and relaxes the cords; ventilation and saturation recover over 1-2 minutes if the spasm was partial or recently complete.

events.label: Succinylcholine + intubation, full recovery
events.description: Succinylcholine 0.5-1 mg/kg IV (with atropine available) breaks refractory spasm; the trachea is intubated and the patient is ventilated with 100% oxygen. Full recovery of oxygenation and ventilation.

events.label: Negative pressure pulmonary edema (optional)
events.description: Minutes after resolution, pink frothy secretions appear in the airway with crackles, tachypnea, and desaturation despite a patent airway — negative pressure pulmonary edema from forceful inspiration against closed cords.

events.label: Full recovery / stabilization
events.description: Oxygenation, ventilation, and hemodynamics return to normal on appropriate support; the team plans disposition and monitoring.

expectedActions.label: Identify elevated laryngospasm risk (recent URI, smoker) and verbalize a plan
Ensure adequate anesthetic depth before instrumenting the airway
Confirm LMA placement with capnography and chest rise
Recognize laryngospasm (stridor/silent chest, lost capnograph, falling SpO2) and announce it
Call for help early in the airway emergency
Apply 100% O2 with sustained CPAP and jaw thrust at Larson's point
expectedActions.description: FiO2 1.0, tight seal, APL 15-20 cmH2O CPAP, firm bilateral pressure in the laryngospasm notch behind the earlobes.

expectedActions.label: Remove the stimulus and deepen anesthesia with propofol 0.5-1 mg/kg IV
Give succinylcholine 0.5-1 mg/kg IV for refractory spasm
expectedActions.description: Small-dose IV succinylcholine (or 4 mg/kg IM if no IV) once CPAP and deepening fail; atropine drawn up for bradycardia.

expectedActions.label: Prepare to intubate (laryngoscope, ETT, suction ready) once paralyzed
Escalate before profound hypoxia; recognize and treat hypoxic bradycardia
expectedActions.description: Does not persist with failing maneuvers while SpO2 falls through the 70s; treats bradycardia by restoring oxygenation, with atropine available.

expectedActions.label: Monitor for negative pressure pulmonary edema after the event
expectedActions.description: Checks for frothy secretions, crackles, and desaturation; plans extended observation and supportive care if present.

expectedProgression: Routine propofol induction and first-pass LMA placement with confirmed ventilation; sevoflurane started.
About five minutes in, at a light plane of anesthesia, partial laryngospasm develops: stridor, shrinking capnograph, SpO2 drifting down.
Learners should announce the diagnosis, switch to 100% O2, apply CPAP with jaw thrust at Larson's point, and stop any stimulation.
If treatment is delayed or the spasm progresses, faculty fires complete laryngospasm: silent chest, EtCO2 zero, SpO2 falling into the 70s.
Deepening with propofol 0.5-1 mg/kg resolves most spasms; refractory spasm requires succinylcholine 0.5-1 mg/kg IV and intubation.
Prolonged hypoxia triggers sinus bradycardia into the 40s, forcing escalation.
After recovery, faculty may fire NPPE; learners should recognize it and plan monitoring and supportive care.
correctManagement: Identify the high-risk airway (recent URI, smoker) preoperatively and ensure adequate depth before airway manipulation.
Diagnose laryngospasm promptly from stridor or silent chest with a lost capnograph trace, and say it out loud to the team.
Immediately deliver 100% oxygen with a tight seal and sustained CPAP of 15-20 cmH2O.
Apply firm jaw thrust with bilateral pressure at Larson's point (the laryngospasm notch).
Remove any stimulus (suction secretions, stop surgical stimulation) and deepen anesthesia with propofol 0.5-1 mg/kg IV.
If spasm is refractory or desaturation is rapid, give succinylcholine 0.5-1 mg/kg IV (4 mg/kg IM if no IV access) without delay.
Prepare and perform tracheal intubation once the cords relax; ventilate with 100% oxygen.
Have atropine ready and treat hypoxic bradycardia primarily by restoring oxygenation.
Call for help early and assign roles (airway, drugs, timer/recorder).
Observe for negative pressure pulmonary edema afterward: frothy secretions, crackles, desaturation; treat with oxygen, positive pressure, and extended monitoring.
commonErrors: Attributing the falling SpO2 to a probe or circuit problem and delaying the diagnosis.
Applying intermittent bag squeezes instead of sustained CPAP with an effective jaw thrust.
Trying to force ventilation against closed cords with high pressures, insufflating the stomach.
Delaying succinylcholine while SpO2 falls through the 70s ('one more try' with CPAP).
Removing the LMA and instrumenting the airway at a light plane, worsening the spasm.
Forgetting atropine/failing to anticipate bradycardia in the hypoxic patient.
Not calling for help until the patient is bradycardic.
Failing to monitor for NPPE after an episode of forceful inspiration against a closed glottis.

points: Laryngospasm risk factors: recent URI (airway hyperreactivity persists 2-6 weeks), smoking, light anesthesia, secretions, and airway manipulation — this patient had four of them.
Partial versus complete spasm: stridor with some CO2 trace versus silent chest with a flat capnograph; the treatment threshold changes accordingly.
First-line sequence: 100% O2, sustained CPAP 15-20 cmH2O, jaw thrust with Larson's point pressure, remove the stimulus.

Pharmacologic escalation: propofol 0.5-1 mg/kg deepens and often breaks the spasm; succinylcholine 0.5-1 mg/kg IV is definitive for refractory spasm and should not be delayed once desaturation is significant.
Hypoxic bradycardia is a pre-arrest rhythm in this setting; the treatment is oxygenation, with atropine as an adjunct.
Negative pressure pulmonary edema follows forceful inspiration against a closed glottis; onset can be delayed, so extended observation is warranted.
Crisis resource management: early call for help, closed-loop communication, and explicit role assignment shorten time to definitive treatment.
questions: What preoperative findings raised this patient's laryngospasm risk, and would they have changed your airway plan?
How did you distinguish partial from complete laryngospasm at the bedside?
Walk me through your first-line maneuvers — what does effective CPAP with a Larson's maneuver actually look and feel like?
What was your trigger point for giving succinylcholine, and in retrospect was it early enough?
How would your management have differed if there had been no IV in place?
What did the bradycardia tell you, and how should it be treated in this context?
What will you watch for over the next few hours after a significant laryngospasm, and why?
